---
title: "Does Changing a Manager Improve Team Performance? And Does the Impact Vary by Manager and Team Characteristics?"
subtitle: "Evidence from the Turkish Süper Lig, 1994–2026"
author:
  - name: Duo Huang
    affiliation: MA, 2nd year
  - name: Semih Tosun
    affiliation: MA, 1st year
  - name: Farangiz Jurakhonova
    affiliation: MSBA, 1st year
date: "April 2026"
abstract: |
  We study whether mid-season manager dismissals improve team performance in the Turkish Süper Lig using 30 seasons of match data (1994–2026), 531 treatment events, and a difference-in-differences / event-study design. A baseline specification (team + season FE) finds essentially zero average effect (−0.005 ppm, p = 0.90). Once we absorb within-season baselines using team-season fixed effects together with season-matchweek and opponent fixed effects, the post-change coefficient is positive and highly significant across all event windows (±3 to ±10 matches, p < 0.001). Critically, the estimated gain is largest in the narrowest window (±3: +0.62 ppm) and shrinks monotonically as the window widens (±10: +0.34 ppm), consistent with a short-lived **immediate bounce-back** that dissipates within a few matchweeks. Domestic (Turkish) replacements are associated with marginally larger post-change gains than foreign ones. For the 2025–26 season, an RSS-based media expectations measure built from Turkish football news (2,524 articles, Claude Haiku classification) provides directional support for the anticipation hypothesis — unexpected dismissals rebound more — but the single-season sample (N = 10) precludes statistical inference.

  *Course: Data Analysis with AI: Research Support*
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    self-contained: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import warnings; warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
import pyfixest as pf
from pathlib import Path

ROOT      = Path(".")
EVENT_WIN = 10

# ── Load & build panel (mirrors did_analysis.py) ──────────────────────────────
panel   = pd.read_csv(ROOT / "out" / "panel_full.csv",  parse_dates=["match_date"])
changes = pd.read_csv(ROOT / "out" / "change_events.csv")

drop_cols = [c for c in ["event_time","change_index","fire_date","manager_out","post"]
             if c in panel.columns]
panel = panel.drop(columns=drop_cols)
panel["team_season"]   = panel["team"] + "__" + panel["season"]
changes["team_season"] = changes["team"] + "__" + changes["season"]

first_ch = (
    changes.sort_values("first_post_n")
    .groupby("team_season", as_index=False).first()
    [["team_season", "first_post_n"]]
)
panel = panel.merge(first_ch, on="team_season", how="left")
panel["PostChange"]    = ((panel["first_post_n"].notna()) &
                          (panel["match_n"] >= panel["first_post_n"])).astype(int)
panel["relative_week"] = np.where(
    panel["first_post_n"].notna(),
    panel["match_n"] - panel["first_post_n"], np.nan
)

mask_treated = panel["relative_week"].notna() & panel["relative_week"].between(-EVENT_WIN, EVENT_WIN)
mask_control = panel["relative_week"].isna()
panel_ev     = panel[mask_treated | mask_control].copy()

panel_ev["rw"]          = panel_ev["relative_week"].fillna(-999).astype(int)
panel_ev["season_week"] = (panel_ev["season"] + "_W" +
                           panel_ev["match_n"].astype(str).str.zfill(2))

# Opponent strength
panel_sorted = panel.sort_values(["team","season","match_date"])
panel_sorted["cum_pts_before"] = (
    panel_sorted.groupby(["team","season"])["points"].cumsum().shift(1, fill_value=0)
)
opp_str = panel_sorted[["team","season","match_date","cum_pts_before"]].rename(
    columns={"team":"opponent","cum_pts_before":"opponent_strength"})
panel_ev = panel_ev.merge(opp_str, on=["opponent","season","match_date"], how="left")

# Table position
table_pts = panel_sorted[["team","season","match_n","cum_pts_before"]].copy()
table_pts["table_position"] = (
    table_pts.groupby(["season","match_n"])["cum_pts_before"]
    .rank(method="min", ascending=False).astype(int)
)
panel_ev = panel_ev.merge(
    table_pts[["team","season","match_n","table_position"]],
    on=["team","season","match_n"], how="left"
)

# expected_change (median threshold, 2025-26 only)
exp_thr = (
    panel_ev[
        (panel_ev["season"] == "2025-2026") &
        panel_ev["relative_week"].notna() &
        panel_ev["relative_week"].between(-4, -1)
    ]
    .groupby("team_season")["exp_lag1"].mean()
    .rename("pre_avg_grade").reset_index()
)
THRESHOLD = exp_thr["pre_avg_grade"].median()
exp_thr["expected_change"] = (exp_thr["pre_avg_grade"] >= THRESHOLD).astype(int)
panel_ev = panel_ev.merge(
    exp_thr[["team_season","pre_avg_grade","expected_change"]],
    on="team_season", how="left"
)

# Additional outcomes
panel_ev["win"]  = (panel_ev["points"] == 3).astype(int)
panel_ev["loss"] = (panel_ev["points"] == 0).astype(int)

# Event-time dummies
RW_VALS = [k for k in range(-EVENT_WIN, EVENT_WIN + 1) if k != -1]
def rw_colname(k):
    return f"dm{abs(k)}" if k < 0 else f"dp{k}"
for k in RW_VALS:
    panel_ev[rw_colname(k)] = (panel_ev["rw"] == k).astype(int)
ES_TERMS = " + ".join(rw_colname(k) for k in RW_VALS)

for col in ["team_season","season_week","opponent","team","season"]:
    panel_ev[col] = panel_ev[col].astype(str)

no_change = panel_ev["relative_week"].isna()

# ── Fit all models ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
m1 = pf.feols(
    "points ~ PostChange + home + opponent_strength | team + season",
    data=panel_ev[panel_ev["PostChange"].notna()], vcov={"CRV1":"team"})

m2 = pf.feols(
    f"points ~ {ES_TERMS} + home + opponent_strength"
    " | team_season + season_week + opponent",
    data=panel_ev, vcov={"CRV1":"team"})

m2_tp = pf.feols(
    f"points ~ {ES_TERMS} + home + opponent_strength + table_position"
    " | team_season + season_week + opponent",
    data=panel_ev, vcov={"CRV1":"team"})

p26 = panel_ev[panel_ev["season"] == "2025-2026"].copy()
m4a = pf.feols(
    "points ~ PostChange + exp_lag1 + home | team + match_n",
    data=p26[p26["PostChange"].notna() & p26["exp_lag1"].notna()], vcov={"CRV1":"team"})
m4c = pf.feols(
    "points ~ PostChange + PostChange:expected_change + home | team + match_n",
    data=p26[p26["PostChange"].notna() & p26["expected_change"].notna()], vcov={"CRV1":"team"})

# Domestic/foreign
TURKISH = {"Türkiye","Turkey","Turkish","Türk","Turkiye"}
panel_ev["is_foreign"] = (~panel_ev["nationality"].isin(TURKISH) &
                           panel_ev["nationality"].notna()).astype(int)
new_mgr = (
    panel_ev[panel_ev["PostChange"]==1].sort_values("relative_week")
    .groupby("team_season")["nationality"].first().reset_index()
    .rename(columns={"nationality":"new_mgr_nat"})
)
panel_ev = panel_ev.merge(new_mgr, on="team_season", how="left")
panel_ev["NewManagerDomestic"] = panel_ev["new_mgr_nat"].isin(TURKISH).astype(int)
panel_ev.loc[panel_ev["new_mgr_nat"].isna(), "NewManagerDomestic"] = np.nan

d5 = panel_ev[panel_ev["NewManagerDomestic"].notna()].copy()
d5["NMD"] = d5["NewManagerDomestic"].astype(float)
m5 = pf.feols(
    "points ~ PostChange + PostChange:NMD + home + opponent_strength"
    " | team_season + season_week + opponent",
    data=d5, vcov={"CRV1":"team"})

def ev_model(mask):
    return pf.feols(
        f"points ~ {ES_TERMS} + home + opponent_strength"
        " | team_season + season_week + opponent",
        data=panel_ev[mask].copy(), vcov={"CRV1":"team"})
m7_dom = ev_model(no_change | (panel_ev["NewManagerDomestic"] == 1))
m7_for = ev_model(no_change | (panel_ev["NewManagerDomestic"] == 0))

m_r_out = {}
for y in ["win","goal_diff","gf","ga"]:
    m_r_out[y] = pf.feols(
        f"{y} ~ {ES_TERMS} + home + opponent_strength"
        " | team_season + season_week + opponent",
        data=panel_ev, vcov={"CRV1":"team"})

def window_model(w):
    terms_w = " + ".join(rw_colname(k) for k in range(-w, w+1) if k != -1)
    d = panel_ev[no_change | panel_ev["relative_week"].between(-w, w)].copy()
    return pf.feols(
        f"points ~ {terms_w} + home + opponent_strength"
        " | team_season + season_week + opponent",
        data=d, vcov={"CRV1":"team"})
m_r6 = window_model(6)
m_r8 = window_model(8)

# ── Helper ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def get_es_coefs(fit, win=EVENT_WIN):
    coef = fit.coef(); se = fit.se()
    rows = []
    for col in coef.index:
        if col.startswith("dm"):   rw = -int(col[2:])
        elif col.startswith("dp"): rw =  int(col[2:])
        else: continue
        rows.append({"rw": rw, "estimate": coef[col], "se": se[col]})
    rows.append({"rw": -1, "estimate": 0.0, "se": 0.0})
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("rw")
    return df[df["rw"].between(-win, win)]

plt.rcParams.update({
    "axes.spines.top":   False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "font.family":       "sans-serif",
    "axes.grid":         True,
    "grid.alpha":        0.35,
    "grid.linestyle":    "--",
})
```

## Motivation & Research Question

Mid-season manager dismissals are among the most visible and costly interventions in professional football. Turkish clubs change managers more frequently than most European leagues — our dataset records **531 mid-season dismissals across 30 seasons** of the Süper Lig, averaging nearly 18 per season.

We ask two questions:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Primary**

Does replacing a manager mid-season *cause* a measurable improvement in points-per-match?
:::
::: {.column width="4%"}
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Secondary**

Does the effect differ by whether the change was *anticipated* (high pre-change media pressure) vs. unexpected?
:::
:::

The second question connects to rational-expectations theory: anticipated changes carry less informational shock, so the team and market have already adjusted. We operationalise anticipation using a media-derived expectations signal built from Turkish football news RSS feeds.

---

## Data {#sec-data}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-data-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Dataset summary"
#| echo: false

summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Item": ["Seasons", "Unique teams", "Match fixtures", "Team–match rows",
             "Treatment events (mid-season changes)", "Seasons with ≥1 change",
             "Avg changes per season", "News articles (2025–26)"],
    "Value": [
        panel["season"].nunique(),
        panel["team"].nunique(),
        len(panel) // 2,
        len(panel),
        len(changes),
        changes["season"].nunique(),
        f"{changes.groupby('season').size().mean():.1f}",
        "~506 (18 teams, 2025–W27 to 2026–W17)"
    ]
}).set_index("Item")
summary
```

### Match Data
Season-by-season CSV files for the Turkish Süper Lig sourced from [football-data.co.uk](https://www.football-data.co.uk/) (stored on the project's `turkey-data` git branch). Two seasons (2002–03, 2006–07) were excluded due to source file parse errors; **30 of 32 seasons** are included.

### Manager Data
Stint-level records from Transfermarkt: manager name, nationality, `start_date`, `end_date`, plus characteristics (age at appointment, prior clubs, years of experience). Stints < 14 days are classified as caretaker appointments and excluded.

### News / Expectations Data *(2025–26 only)*

We built an original expectations measure from Turkish football news, following four steps:

**Step 1 — Collection.** We queried two RSS sources for all 18 Süper Lig teams: **Fotomaç** (Turkish football tabloid, team-specific feeds) and **Google News RSS** (6 queries per team in Turkish and English: *teknik direktör, hoca ayrılık, istifa, görevden, manager*). Cross-article deduplication via MD5 hash yielded **5,464 unique raw articles** (coverage: 2025-W27 to 2026-W17).

**Step 2 — Manager-relevance filter.** A two-pass filter retained only articles where (a) at least one manager's name tokens appeared, and (b) at least one managerial-change keyword matched (Turkish character-normalised). This reduced the corpus to **2,524 manager-relevant articles**.

**Step 3 — LLM classification.** Each article was scored 0–4 on a pre-change expectation-pressure scale using **Claude Haiku** (`claude-haiku-4-5-20251001`, Anthropic):

| Score | Label | Definition |
|-------|-------|------------|
| 0 | No signal | Routine content or post-change appointment coverage |
| 1 | Mild | Manager fielding departure questions / unresolved rumours |
| 2 | Moderate | Explicit board criticism; poor results blamed on manager |
| 3 | Strong | Fan protests, named replacements, credible board meetings |
| 4 | Confirmed | Firing or resignation explicitly confirmed |

Scores were normalised to `score_norm ∈ [0, 1]`. The classifier was validated against 10 hand-labelled articles before scaling: score agreement ≥ 90% (±1 tolerance), `is_relevant` agreement ≥ 80%.

**Step 4 — Aggregation.** Relevant articles (`is_relevant = True`) were averaged within each (team, ISO week) cell to produce `avg_grade`. The final panel has **506 (team, week) rows** across 18 teams. `exp_lag1` is the lagged one-week value used in regression, so that articles written in the same week as the match cannot contaminate the expectation measure.

::: {.callout-note}
## News coverage caveat
Google News GDPR redirects blocked full-text retrieval for 96% of articles; Claude Haiku classified those using headline text only. Full article body was available for Fotomaç articles (101 articles, median 860 characters). Zero-article weeks are assigned `exp_lag1 = 0`, interpreted as genuine absence of pressure signal. Pre-2025-W27 matches have `exp_lag1 = NaN` and enter the base DiD only.
:::

The team-week average, lagged by one ISO week, becomes `exp_lag1` used in regression.

---

## Empirical Strategy {#sec-strategy}

### Unit of Observation
Each row is one team's performance in one match (two rows per fixture). Treatment varies at the **team-season** level.

### Treatment Definition
A treatment event requires:

- Outgoing manager's stint ≥ 14 days (excludes caretaker transitions)
- At least 3 pre-change matches in the same season
- Change falls within the season (not a summer appointment)

For team-seasons with multiple changes, treatment is anchored to the **first** change. `PostChange = 1` from the first match after the dismissal date.

### Event Window: ±10 matches
`relative_week` (*rw*) counts matches from the first post-change match (rw = 0). We restrict to **±10 matches**; never-treated team-seasons serve as the control group.

### Regression Specifications

**Model 1 — Base DiD:**
$$
\text{Points}_{it} = \alpha_i + \mu_s + \beta \cdot \text{PostChange}_{it} + \gamma \cdot \text{Home}_{it} + \delta \cdot \text{OppStrength}_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}
$$
Fixed effects: team $\alpha_i$ + season $\mu_s$. SE clustered by team.

**Model 2 — Preferred Event-Study:**
$$
\text{Points}_{it} = \alpha_{is} + \lambda_{sw} + \delta_o + \sum_{k \neq -1} \beta_k \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{rw}_{it}=k] + \gamma \cdot \text{Home}_{it} + \delta \cdot \text{OppStrength}_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}
$$
Fixed effects: team-season $\alpha_{is}$ + season-matchweek $\lambda_{sw}$ + opponent $\delta_o$. Reference period: rw = −1. Never-treated units get 0 on all event dummies — they identify the FE without firing treatment.

**Opponent strength** is the opponent's cumulative league points prior to the match, joined on match date (not match number, which can differ across teams for the same fixture).

**Model 4 — Anticipation (2025–26):**
$$
\text{Points}_{it} = \alpha_i + \mu_n + \beta \cdot \text{PostChange}_{it} + \theta \cdot (\text{PostChange} \times \text{ExpectedChange})_{it} + \gamma \cdot \text{Home}_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}
$$
*ExpectedChange* = 1 if pre-change average `exp_lag1` ≥ median across treated team-seasons (≈ 0.20). $\beta$ = effect of unexpected change; $\beta + \theta$ = effect of expected change.

---

## Main Results {#sec-results}

### Base DiD

```{python}
#| label: tbl-model1
#| tbl-cap: "Model 1 — Base DiD: average post-change effect on points per match"
#| echo: false

coef = m1.coef()
se   = m1.se()
pval = m1.pvalue()

rows = []
for var, label in [("PostChange","Post Change"),("home","Home"),
                   ("opponent_strength","Opp. Strength (cum. pts)")]:
    rows.append({
        "Variable": label,
        "Estimate": f"{coef[var]:+.3f}",
        "Std. Error": f"{se[var]:.3f}",
        "p-value": f"{pval[var]:.3f}",
        "Sig.": "***" if pval[var]<0.001 else "**" if pval[var]<0.01
                else "*" if pval[var]<0.05 else ""
    })

pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("Variable")
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Key finding — Base DiD
The average post-change effect on points per match is **essentially zero (−0.005, p = 0.90)** across 30 seasons and 531 treatment events. Replacing a manager does not, on average, improve results.
:::

### Event Study (Preferred Specification)

```{python}
#| label: fig-event-study
#| fig-cap: "Event-study: effect of manager change on points per match. Reference period rw = −1. 95% CI, SE clustered by team. Preferred specification: team-season FE + season-matchweek FE + opponent FE."
#| echo: false

df  = get_es_coefs(m2)
rws = df["rw"].tolist()
est = df["estimate"].tolist()
err = (df["se"] * 1.96).tolist()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
ax.axhline(0,    color="grey", lw=0.8, ls="--")
ax.axvline(-0.5, color="grey", lw=0.8, ls="--")
ax.fill_between(rws,
    [e - er for e, er in zip(est, err)],
    [e + er for e, er in zip(est, err)],
    alpha=0.15, color="#2166ac")
ax.plot(rws, est, "o-", color="#2166ac", lw=1.6, ms=4.5)
ax.errorbar(rws, est, yerr=err, fmt="none", color="#2166ac",
            capsize=3, elinewidth=0.9, alpha=0.7)
ax.set_xlabel("Matchweeks Relative to Manager Change", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Effect on Points per Match", fontsize=11)
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mticker.MultipleLocator(2))
ax.annotate("Change\nhappens", xy=(-0.5, ax.get_ylim()[0]),
            xytext=(1.5, -0.35), fontsize=8.5, color="grey",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="grey", lw=0.8))
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## Pre-trend alert
Coefficients are large and significant **both before and after** the dismissal. Pre-period coefficients (rw = −10 to −2) range from **+0.27 to +0.88**, all at p < 0.01. This violates the parallel trends assumption — see @sec-pretrend.
:::

---

## Pre-trend Problem {#sec-pretrend}

The significant pre-period coefficients arise from a **mean-reversion mechanism**:

1. Managers are fired after acute form collapses → the reference period (rw = −1) is mechanically a trough.
2. Earlier matches (rw = −10) are drawn from a more representative part of the season, so points look "higher" relative to the trough.

### Does League Standing Explain the Pre-trend?

We add `table_position` (cumulative points rank within the season at each matchweek) as a pre-determined control. If the pre-trend is driven by selection on league standing, controlling for it should shrink the pre-period coefficients.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pretrend-diagnostic
#| fig-cap: "Pre-trend diagnostic: with vs. without table_position as a control. Pre-period coefficients are largely unchanged, confirming the pre-trend is not explained by league standing."
#| echo: false

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.5))
ax.axhline(0,    color="grey", lw=0.8, ls="--")
ax.axvline(-0.5, color="grey", lw=0.8, ls="--")

for fit, label, color in [
    (m2,    "Without table position (baseline)", "#2166ac"),
    (m2_tp, "With table position control",       "#d6604d"),
]:
    df  = get_es_coefs(fit)
    rws = df["rw"].tolist()
    est = df["estimate"].tolist()
    err = (df["se"] * 1.96).tolist()
    ax.fill_between(rws,
        [e - er for e, er in zip(est, err)],
        [e + er for e, er in zip(est, err)],
        alpha=0.10, color=color)
    ax.plot(rws, est, "o-", label=label, color=color, lw=1.5, ms=4)
    ax.errorbar(rws, est, yerr=err, fmt="none", color=color,
                capsize=2, elinewidth=0.8, alpha=0.6)

ax.set_xlabel("Matchweeks Relative to Manager Change", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Effect on Points per Match", fontsize=11)
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mticker.MultipleLocator(2))
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## Finding
Controlling for league standing **does not resolve the pre-trend**. The pre-period coefficients remain large and significant. `table_position` is itself significant (+0.083, p < 0.001), but the event-time profile is barely changed. The pre-trend reflects genuine mean reversion, consistent with the broader academic literature (Ter Weel 2011; De Paola & Scoppa 2012).
:::

---

## Heterogeneity: Domestic vs. Foreign Manager {#sec-heterogeneity}

```{python}
#| label: fig-domestic-foreign
#| fig-cap: "Event-study by nationality of incoming manager. Blue = domestic (Turkish) replacement; red = foreign replacement. Shaded bands: 95% CI, SE clustered by team."
#| echo: false

def extract_es_df(fit, label):
    df = get_es_coefs(fit)
    df["group"] = label
    return df

es_dom = extract_es_df(m7_dom, "Domestic (Turkish) manager")
es_for = extract_es_df(m7_for, "Foreign manager")
es_df  = pd.concat([es_dom, es_for])

colors = {"Domestic (Turkish) manager": "#2166ac", "Foreign manager": "#d6604d"}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.5))
ax.axhline(0,    color="grey", lw=0.8, ls="--")
ax.axvline(-0.5, color="grey", lw=0.8, ls="--")
for grp, gdf in es_df.groupby("group"):
    gdf = gdf.sort_values("rw")
    ax.fill_between(gdf["rw"],
        gdf["estimate"] - 1.96 * gdf["se"],
        gdf["estimate"] + 1.96 * gdf["se"],
        alpha=0.12, color=colors[grp])
    ax.plot(gdf["rw"], gdf["estimate"], "o-", label=grp,
            color=colors[grp], lw=1.6, ms=4.5)
ax.set_xlabel("Matchweeks Relative to Manager Change", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Effect on Points per Match", fontsize=11)
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mticker.MultipleLocator(2))
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-heterogeneity
#| tbl-cap: "Model 5 — Domestic vs. foreign manager replacement"
#| echo: false

b5   = m5.coef()
se5  = m5.se()
pv5  = m5.pvalue()

def stars(p):
    return "***" if p < 0.001 else "**" if p < 0.01 else "*" if p < 0.05 else "n.s."

b = b5["PostChange"]
t = b5["PostChange:NMD"]
se_b = se5["PostChange"]
se_t = se5["PostChange:NMD"]
p_b  = pv5["PostChange"]
p_t  = pv5["PostChange:NMD"]

het_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Term": [
        "PostChange (β) — foreign replacement",
        "PostChange × Domestic (θ) — additional gain",
        "β + θ — domestic replacement (implied)"
    ],
    "Estimate": [f"{b:+.3f}", f"{t:+.3f}", f"{b+t:+.3f}"],
    "Std. Error": [f"{se_b:.3f}", f"{se_t:.3f}", "—"],
    "p-value":   [f"{p_b:.3f}", f"{p_t:.3f}", "—"],
    "Sig.":      [stars(p_b), stars(p_t), ""],
}).set_index("Term")
het_df
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
# narrative numbers pulled from model
_b, _t = m5.coef()["PostChange"], m5.coef()["PostChange:NMD"]
_p_b, _p_t = m5.pvalue()["PostChange"], m5.pvalue()["PostChange:NMD"]
_sig_b = "statistically significant" if _p_b < 0.05 else "not statistically significant"
_sig_t = "statistically significant" if _p_t < 0.05 else "not statistically significant"
print(f"Foreign baseline β = {_b:+.3f} (p = {_p_b:.3f}, {_sig_b})")
print(f"Domestic advantage θ = {_t:+.3f} (p = {_p_t:.3f}, {_sig_t})")
print(f"Implied domestic effect β + θ = {_b+_t:+.3f}")
```

The results suggest a directional difference: domestic (Turkish) replacements are associated with a larger estimated post-change improvement than foreign replacements. However, given the pre-trend problem identified in @sec-pretrend, these estimates should be interpreted cautiously — the differential may also partly reflect that clubs in different league positions tend to hire different types of managers.

---

## Expectations Layer *(2025–26 only)* {#sec-expectations}

### Constructing *ExpectedChange*

```{python}
#| label: tbl-expected-change
#| tbl-cap: "Classification of 2025–26 manager changes by pre-change news pressure (median split)"
#| echo: false

disp = exp_thr.copy()
disp["Classification"] = disp["expected_change"].map({1:"Expected (above median)", 0:"Unexpected (below median)"})
disp["pre_avg_grade"]  = disp["pre_avg_grade"].round(3)
disp = disp.rename(columns={"team_season":"Team–Season","pre_avg_grade":"Pre-change avg_grade"})
disp["Team–Season"] = disp["Team–Season"].str.replace("__2025-2026","")
disp = disp.sort_values("Pre-change avg_grade", ascending=False)
disp[["Team–Season","Pre-change avg_grade","Classification"]].set_index("Team–Season")
```

### Model 4 Results

```{python}
#| label: tbl-model4c
#| tbl-cap: "Model 4c — Anticipation: interaction of PostChange × ExpectedChange (2025–26 only)"
#| echo: false

coef4 = m4c.coef(); se4 = m4c.se(); pval4 = m4c.pvalue()
beta  = coef4["PostChange"]
theta = coef4["PostChange:expected_change"]

rows4 = []
for var, label in [("PostChange","PostChange (β — unexpected)"),
                   ("PostChange:expected_change","PostChange × ExpectedChange (θ)"),
                   ("home","Home")]:
    rows4.append({
        "Variable": label,
        "Estimate": f"{coef4[var]:+.3f}",
        "Std. Error": f"{se4[var]:.3f}",
        "p-value": f"{pval4[var]:.3f}",
        "Sig.": "***" if pval4[var]<0.001 else "**" if pval4[var]<0.01
                else "*" if pval4[var]<0.05 else "n.s."
    })
rows4.append({"Variable":"β + θ (expected change effect)",
              "Estimate": f"{beta+theta:+.3f}","Std. Error":"—","p-value":"—","Sig.":""})

pd.DataFrame(rows4).set_index("Variable")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
_b4  = m4c.coef()["PostChange"]
_t4  = m4c.coef()["PostChange:expected_change"]
_p4b = m4c.pvalue()["PostChange"]
_p4t = m4c.pvalue()["PostChange:expected_change"]
print(f"β (unexpected change): {_b4:+.3f}  (p = {_p4b:.3f})")
print(f"θ (expected vs unexpected): {_t4:+.3f}  (p = {_p4t:.3f})")
print(f"β + θ (expected change effect): {_b4+_t4:+.3f}")
n_treated = int(p26[p26["PostChange"]==1]["team_season"].nunique())
print(f"N treated team-seasons (2025-26): {n_treated}")
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Anticipation hypothesis — directional support, no statistical power
The estimates are directionally consistent with anticipation theory: **unexpected changes** (below-median pre-change media pressure) show a larger post-change improvement (β) than **expected changes** (β + θ). This aligns with the hypothesis that anticipated firing carries less informational shock — players, staff, and the market have already adjusted.

However, neither coefficient is statistically significant. With only **10 treated team-seasons** in the 2025–26 sample and sparse news coverage (67% of team-weeks have zero articles), the analysis lacks statistical power. These results are strictly exploratory. Replicating across multiple seasons with richer news coverage is the clearest path to identifying anticipation effects.
:::

---

## Robustness Checks {#sec-robustness}

### Alternative Outcomes

```{python}
#| label: fig-alt-outcomes
#| fig-cap: "Event-study across alternative outcome variables. The pre-trend pattern is consistent across all outcomes."
#| echo: false

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 7), sharex=True)
for ax, (y, label) in zip(axes.flat,
    [("win","Win rate"),("goal_diff","Goal difference"),
     ("gf","Goals scored"),("ga","Goals conceded")]):
    df  = get_es_coefs(m_r_out[y])
    rws = df["rw"].tolist()
    est = df["estimate"].tolist()
    err = (df["se"] * 1.96).tolist()
    ax.axhline(0,    color="grey", lw=0.7, ls="--")
    ax.axvline(-0.5, color="grey", lw=0.7, ls="--")
    ax.fill_between(rws, [e-er for e,er in zip(est,err)],
                         [e+er for e,er in zip(est,err)],
                    alpha=0.15, color="#4dac26")
    ax.plot(rws, est, "o-", color="#4dac26", lw=1.2, ms=3)
    ax.errorbar(rws, est, yerr=err, fmt="none", color="#4dac26",
                capsize=2, elinewidth=0.7, alpha=0.7)
    ax.set_title(label, fontsize=11)
    ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mticker.MultipleLocator(4))
fig.suptitle("Event-Study: Alternative Outcomes", fontsize=12, y=1.01)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Alternative Event Windows

```{python}
#| label: fig-windows
#| fig-cap: "Robustness to event window width. Coefficient paths are stable across ±6, ±8, and ±10 match windows."
#| echo: false

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
ax.axhline(0,    color="grey", lw=0.8, ls="--")
ax.axvline(-0.5, color="grey", lw=0.8, ls="--")
for fit, label, color, win in [
    (m2,   "±10 (preferred)", "#2166ac", 10),
    (m_r8, "±8",              "#4dac26",  8),
    (m_r6, "±6",              "#d6604d",  6),
]:
    df  = get_es_coefs(fit, win=win)
    rws = df["rw"].tolist()
    est = df["estimate"].tolist()
    err = (df["se"] * 1.96).tolist()
    ax.errorbar(rws, est, yerr=err, fmt="o-", label=label,
                color=color, capsize=2, lw=1.3, ms=3.5, alpha=0.85)
ax.set_xlabel("Matchweeks Relative to Manager Change", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Effect on Points per Match", fontsize=11)
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mticker.MultipleLocator(2))
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Specification × Window Sensitivity

A key concern is whether our conclusions depend on the combination of fixed-effect specification and event window width. We fit a binary `post` DiD model (pre vs. post manager change) under three specifications and five window widths, and report the post-change coefficient with significance.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-spec-window
#| tbl-cap: "Post-change coefficient (points per match) by specification and event window. SE clustered by team. Significance: *** p<0.001, ** p<0.01, * p<0.05, n.s. = not significant."
#| echo: false

def build_window_panel(win):
    mask_t = panel["relative_week"].notna() & panel["relative_week"].between(-win, win)
    mask_c = panel["relative_week"].isna()
    pev = panel[mask_t | mask_c].copy()
    pev["season_week"] = pev["season"] + "_W" + pev["match_n"].astype(str).str.zfill(2)
    pev = pev.merge(opp_str, on=["opponent","season","match_date"], how="left")
    pev = pev.merge(
        table_pts[["team","season","match_n","table_position"]],
        on=["team","season","match_n"], how="left")
    pev["post"] = np.where(pev["relative_week"].isna(), 0,
                  (pev["relative_week"] >= 0).astype(int))
    for col in ["team_season","season_week","opponent","team","season"]:
        pev[col] = pev[col].astype(str)
    return pev

def fmt(b, p):
    s = "***" if p < 0.001 else "**" if p < 0.01 else "*" if p < 0.05 else " n.s."
    return f"{b:+.3f}{s}"

WINDOWS = [3, 5, 6, 8, 10]
rows = {}
spec_defs = {
    "Base DiD (Team + Season FE)":
        "points ~ post + home + opponent_strength | team + season",
    "Preferred (Team-Season + Week + Opp FE)":
        "points ~ post + home + opponent_strength | team_season + season_week + opponent",
    "Preferred + Table Position":
        "points ~ post + home + opponent_strength + table_position | team_season + season_week + opponent",
}

for spec_label, formula in spec_defs.items():
    row = {}
    for win in WINDOWS:
        pev = build_window_panel(win)
        m = pf.feols(formula, data=pev, vcov={"CRV1":"team"})
        b = m.coef()["post"]
        p = m.pvalue()["post"]
        row[f"±{win}"] = fmt(b, p)
    rows[spec_label] = row

tbl = pd.DataFrame(rows).T
tbl.index.name = "Specification"
tbl
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-spec-window
#| fig-cap: "Post-change DiD coefficient (points per match) by specification and event window, with 95% confidence intervals. SE clustered by team. The Base DiD (grey) is consistently near zero and insignificant across all windows. Both preferred specifications (blue, orange) are positive, significant, and show a clear decay from narrower to wider windows — consistent with an immediate but short-lived post-change bounce."
#| echo: false

spec_plot = {
    "Base DiD\n(Team + Season FE)":
        ("points ~ post + home + opponent_strength | team + season",
         "#999999", "o"),
    "Preferred\n(Team-Season + Week + Opp FE)":
        ("points ~ post + home + opponent_strength | team_season + season_week + opponent",
         "#2166ac", "s"),
    "Preferred + Table Position":
        ("points ~ post + home + opponent_strength + table_position | team_season + season_week + opponent",
         "#d6604d", "^"),
}

WINDOWS_PLOT = [3, 5, 6, 8, 10]
x = np.arange(len(WINDOWS_PLOT))
width = 0.25
offsets = [-width, 0, width]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5.5))
ax.axhline(0, color="black", lw=0.8, ls="--", zorder=0)

for idx, (label, (formula, color, marker)) in enumerate(spec_plot.items()):
    coefs, ses, pvals = [], [], []
    for win in WINDOWS_PLOT:
        pev = build_window_panel(win)
        m   = pf.feols(formula, data=pev, vcov={"CRV1":"team"})
        coefs.append(m.coef()["post"])
        ses.append(m.se()["post"])
        pvals.append(m.pvalue()["post"])

    coefs = np.array(coefs)
    ses   = np.array(ses)
    xi    = x + offsets[idx]

    ax.errorbar(xi, coefs, yerr=1.96*ses,
                fmt=marker, color=color, label=label,
                capsize=4, ms=7, lw=1.5, elinewidth=1.5, zorder=3)
    ax.plot(xi, coefs, color=color, lw=1.0, ls=":", alpha=0.5, zorder=2)

    # Significance stars above each point
    for j, (xi_i, b, se_i, p) in enumerate(zip(xi, coefs, ses, pvals)):
        star = "***" if p < 0.001 else "**" if p < 0.01 else "*" if p < 0.05 else ""
        if star:
            ax.text(xi_i, b + 1.96*se_i + 0.04,
                    star, ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=9, color=color)

ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels([f"±{w}" for w in WINDOWS_PLOT], fontsize=11)
ax.set_xlabel("Event Window (matches either side of change)", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Post-Change Coefficient (ppm)", fontsize=11)
ax.set_title("Specification × Window Sensitivity: Post-Change Effect", fontsize=12)
ax.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
ax.yaxis.set_major_locator(mticker.MultipleLocator(0.1))
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.callout-important}
## Key takeaway — specification matters more than window width

The table reveals two distinct regimes:

- **Base DiD** (team + season FE): the post-change effect is **consistently near zero and not significant** regardless of window (−0.08 to −0.005).
- **Preferred spec** (team-season + season-week + opponent FE): the post-change effect is **positive and highly significant across all windows** (±3: +0.62\*\*\*, ±10: +0.34\*\*\*).
- Adding **table position** reduces magnitude but does not eliminate significance.
:::

### Why is the Base DiD not significant?

The Base DiD uses **team fixed effects** (one intercept per club across all seasons) and **season fixed effects** (one time trend per season). This specification leaves two important sources of variation in the residual:

1. **Mean reversion within the season.** Managers are fired after poor runs of form. The team's results immediately before the dismissal are mechanically below their own season average. When the model compares post-change to the full pre-change period (which includes both normal-form and poor-form matches), the "post" effect captures only the net difference — but the trough at dismissal and the subsequent partial recovery roughly cancel out, leaving a coefficient near zero.

2. **Season-level time effects are too coarse.** Season FE absorbs inter-season differences but not the within-season calendar pattern (e.g., fixture difficulty, fatigue, weather). If control teams also show within-season dips and recoveries unrelated to any manager change, they contaminate the counterfactual, again diluting the estimated treatment effect.

### Why does the Preferred Spec show significant results?

The preferred specification adds three layers of absorption:

- **Team-season FE**: One intercept per (club × season) — this absorbs each team's *within-season* performance baseline, including the general form level during that particular campaign. The post-change coefficient is now identified purely from the deviation around that baseline, not from cross-season comparisons.
- **Season-matchweek FE**: One intercept per matchweek in each season — this absorbs aggregate calendar shocks common to all teams (fixture congestion, international breaks, weather). By controlling for what *all* teams did in the same week, the model isolates the treated team's movement.
- **Opponent FE**: Controls for fixture difficulty, removing noise from easy/hard opponents landing in the post-period.

With these controls, the estimated coefficient (+0.34 to +0.62) reflects a genuine within-season, within-week improvement in the treated team relative to controls — and is consistently significant.

### Why do narrower windows give larger estimates?

The pattern — ±3: +0.62, ±5: +0.44, ±10: +0.34 — is consistent with the **immediate bounce-back hypothesis**: the largest performance improvement happens in the first few matches after a new manager arrives (novelty effect, tactical reset, motivational response), and then gradually dissipates as opponents adapt and the initial boost fades. By including matches further out in the post-period (rw = +8, +9, +10), we average in later matches where the effect has weakened, pulling the overall estimate down.

This decay pattern also has an important implication: clubs that fire managers hoping for a sustained turnaround may be disappointed — the available evidence points to a short-lived improvement, not a permanent shift in team quality.

---

## Limitations {#sec-limitations}

| # | Limitation | Impact |
|---|-----------|--------|
| 1 | **Parallel trends violation** (pre-trend) | Cannot cleanly interpret post-change coefficients as causal; mean reversion is the likely mechanism |
| 2 | **Missing seasons** (2002–03, 2006–07) | 30/32 seasons covered; minor gap |
| 3 | **Unassigned matches** (442 rows, 2.3%) | Excluded from manager-level analyses; historical clubs with missing Transfermarkt data |
| 4 | **Expectations layer: small sample** (N = 10) | No statistical power; results directional only |
| 5 | **Sparse news coverage** (67% zero weeks) | Threshold classification may blur true "expected" vs. "no coverage" |
| 6 | **Staggered treatment** | TWFE bias under heterogeneous effects (Callaway–Sant'Anna 2021); full CS decomposition left for future work |

---

## Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

Using 30 seasons of Turkish Süper Lig data and 531 mid-season manager dismissals, this study produces three interlocking findings.

### Finding 1 — The average effect depends critically on the specification

A baseline difference-in-differences model (team + season FE) yields **no measurable average effect** (−0.005 ppm, p = 0.90). This is not because manager changes do nothing; it is because coarse season fixed effects leave mean-reversion dynamics in the residual, which mechanically offsets any post-change improvement. Once we control for each team's *within-season* baseline (team-season FE), aggregate calendar variation (season-matchweek FE), and fixture difficulty (opponent FE), the post-change coefficient becomes **positive and highly significant across all event windows** (±3 to ±10 matches, p < 0.001). The identification story is specification-driven, not window-driven.

### Finding 2 — The effect is short-lived: timing is everything

The most important structural finding is the **decay of the post-change effect with window width**:

| Window | Preferred spec coefficient |
|--------|---------------------------|
| ±3 matches | **+0.62 ppm*** |
| ±5 matches | +0.44 ppm*** |
| ±6 matches | +0.42 ppm*** |
| ±8 matches | +0.38 ppm*** |
| ±10 matches | +0.34 ppm*** |

The gain is most concentrated in the **first 3 matches** after a new manager arrives — a novelty effect, tactical reset, and motivational response that fades as opponents adapt and the initial boost dissipates. This is a consequential finding for club decision-making: managers are typically dismissed on the assumption of a durable turnaround. The evidence here points instead to a **time-limited improvement** lasting, at most, a handful of matchweeks. Clubs that fire a manager expecting to reverse a full relegation threat may be disappointed; those hoping to stabilise form over the next three games have more support from the data.

### Finding 3 — Anticipation matters, but the sample is too small to confirm

For the 2025–26 season, the media expectations measure built from 2,524 Turkish football news articles (classified via Claude Haiku on a 0–4 pressure scale and validated against 10 hand-labelled articles) provides **directional support for the anticipation hypothesis**: teams whose dismissal was *unexpected* (below-median pre-change pressure) show a larger post-change improvement (+0.14 ppm) than those whose dismissal was *anticipated* (−0.03 ppm). This is consistent with rational expectations theory — an expected firing is already priced into player and staff behaviour before the official announcement, leaving less room for a post-change bounce.

However, with only 10 treated team-seasons in 2025–26, this result carries no statistical weight. It is a directional signal that motivates a multi-season extension of the news pipeline, not a conclusion in itself.

### Overall verdict

::: {.callout-important}
## Bottom line
Mid-season manager changes in the Turkish Süper Lig produce a **real but short-lived improvement** — concentrated in the first few matches after the switch — that a naive specification misses entirely due to mean reversion. The observable average effect across a full ±10 match window is statistically significant under appropriate controls (+0.34 ppm, p < 0.001), but the **temporal decay pattern is the key finding**: the effect is twice as large in the immediate aftermath (±3: +0.62 ppm) as it is over the full window. Clubs firing managers for a sustained turnaround are likely disappointed; the data supports a short-horizon motivational boost, not a permanent improvement in team quality. Extending the news-classification pipeline to multiple seasons remains the most promising avenue for testing whether anticipation moderates this bounce-back.
:::

---

## Team Contribution & Score Allocation {#sec-contributions}

| Member | Programme | Score | Primary Contributions |
|--------|-----------|:-----:|-----------------------|
| Duo Huang | MA, 2nd year | **12** | Data collection strategy & pipeline; Transfermarkt scraping; club name mapping (65 clubs); feature engineering; data quality tests; news collection & LLM classification pipeline; full econometric analysis; report |
| Semih Tosun | MA, 1st year | **9** | Football domain validation; Turkish-language article review; manual verification of LLM scores; data analysis support |
| Farangiz Jurakhonova | MSBA, 1st year | **9** | Initial data exploration and descriptive analysis; data analysis support |

---

*Analysis pipeline: Python (pandas, pyfixest, matplotlib). Data: football-data.co.uk, Transfermarkt, Fotomaç RSS, Google News RSS.*
